mergeRationale: The change lets an undefined key reach jwt.verify and drops the algorithm allowlist, so verification can fail open and accept forged tokens depending on the jsonwebtoken version.

requiredFindings.summary: The patch removes the algorithms allowlist and lets an undefined secret reach jwt.verify.
requiredFindings.expectedReasoning: Authentication must fail closed. With the algorithm no longer pinned, verify trusts the token's own alg header; combined with a controllable or undefined key this enables alg:none or HS/RS confusion (using the RS256 public key as an HMAC secret) to forge tokens, depending on the library version.
requiredFindings.acceptableFix: Validate required keys at startup (fail closed), keep algorithms pinned to RS256, and upgrade to a patched jsonwebtoken version.

requiredFindings.summary: The added test only covers a dev happy path, not malicious or misconfigured tokens.
requiredFindings.expectedReasoning: Auth changes need negative tests: alg:none, wrong algorithm, missing key, wrong issuer and wrong audience, plus a startup-configuration test.
requiredFindings.acceptableFix: Add negative token tests and a startup configuration (fail-closed) test.

optionalFindings.summary: process.env.JWT_SECRET || undefined silently degrades a missing configuration into an undefined key instead of failing at startup.

disallowedConclusions: The patch can be merged because the local dev test passes and issuer/audience are still checked.
Reading the key from JWT_SECRET is fine because production still sets a strong secret.
